mn22:1.2: At one time, the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn22:10.1: Here, *bhikkhus*, some foolish men learn the *dhamma*—
mn22:10.10: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a man wanted a snake, was seeking a snake, and was wandering in search of a snake.
mn22:10.11: He might see a large snake.
mn22:10.12: He might seize it by the coils or by the tail.
mn22:10.13: Then that snake, twisting back, might bite him on the hand or arm or on some other limb.
mn22:10.14: Because of that, he might meet with death or deadly *dukkha*.
mn22:10.16: Because, *bhikkhus*, the snake was wrongly grasped.
mn22:10.17: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, here some foolish men learn the *dhamma*—
mn22:10.18: discourses, mixed prose and verse, expositions, verses, inspired utterances, “thus-said” sayings, birth stories, marvels, and question-and-answer texts.
mn22:10.19: Having learned that *dhamma*, they do not examine the meaning of those *dhammas* with discernment [*paññāya*].
mn22:10.2: discourses, mixed prose and verse, expositions, verses, inspired utterances, “thus-said” sayings, birth stories, marvels, and question-and-answer texts.
mn22:10.20: Since the meaning of those *dhammas* is not examined with discernment [*paññāya*], they do not stand up to reflection.
mn22:10.21: They learn the *dhamma* both for the sake of criticizing others and for the sake of winning arguments.
mn22:10.22: And they do not experience the purpose for which they learn the *dhamma*.
mn22:10.23: Those *dhammas*, wrongly grasped by them, lead for a long time to harm and *dukkha*.
mn22:10.3: Having learned that *dhamma*, they do not examine the meaning of those *dhammas* with discernment [*paññāya*].
mn22:10.4: Since the meaning of those *dhammas* is not examined with discernment [*paññāya*], they do not stand up to reflection.
mn22:10.5: They learn the *dhamma* both for the sake of criticizing others and for the sake of winning arguments.
mn22:10.6: And they do not experience the purpose for which they learn the *dhamma*.
mn22:10.7: Those *dhammas*, wrongly grasped by them, lead for a long time to harm and *dukkha*.
mn22:11.1: But here, *bhikkhus*, some sons of good families learn the *dhamma*—
mn22:11.10: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a man wanted a snake, was seeking a snake, and was wandering in search of a snake.
mn22:11.11: He might see a large snake.
mn22:11.12: He might pin it down well with a forked stick.
mn22:11.13: Having pinned it down well with a forked stick, he might grasp it well by the neck.
mn22:11.14: Even though that snake might coil itself around that man’s hand or arm or some other limb, still because of that he would not meet with death or deadly *dukkha*.
mn22:11.16: Because, *bhikkhus*, the snake was well grasped.
mn22:11.17: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, here some sons of good families learn the *dhamma*—
mn22:11.18: discourses, mixed prose and verse, expositions, verses, inspired utterances, “thus-said” sayings, birth stories, marvels, and question-and-answer texts.
mn22:11.19: Having learned that *dhamma*, they examine the meaning of those *dhammas* with discernment [*paññāya*].
mn22:11.2: discourses, mixed prose and verse, expositions, verses, inspired utterances, “thus-said” sayings, birth stories, marvels, and question-and-answer texts.
mn22:11.20: Since the meaning of those *dhammas* has been examined with discernment [*paññāya*], they stand up to reflection.
mn22:11.21: They do not learn the *dhamma* for the sake of criticizing others, nor for the sake of winning arguments.
mn22:11.22: And they experience the purpose for which they learn the *dhamma*.
mn22:11.23: Those *dhammas*, well grasped by them, lead for a long time to benefit, welfare, and *sukha*.
mn22:11.3: Having learned that *dhamma*, they examine the meaning of those *dhammas* with discernment [*paññāya*].
mn22:11.4: Since the meaning of those *dhammas* has been examined with discernment [*paññāya*], they stand up to reflection.
mn22:11.5: They do not learn the *dhamma* for the sake of criticizing others or for the sake of winning arguments.
mn22:11.6: And they experience the purpose for which they learn the *dhamma*.
mn22:11.7: Those *dhammas*, well grasped by them, lead for a long time to benefit and *sukha*.
mn22:12.1: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, when you understand the meaning of what I have said, you should retain it accordingly.
mn22:12.2: But when you do not understand the meaning of what I have said, then you should question me about it, or those *bhikkhus* who are skilled.
mn22:13.1: I will teach you the *dhamma* by the simile of a raft, *bhikkhus*, for the purpose of crossing over, not for the purpose of grasping.
mn22:13.10: But there is no boat or bridge for going from this shore to the far shore.
mn22:13.11: Why don’t I gather grass, sticks, branches, and leaves, bind them together into a raft, and relying on that raft, striving with hands and feet, cross safely to the far shore?’
mn22:13.12: Then, *bhikkhus*, that man would gather grass, sticks, branches, and leaves, bind them together into a raft, and relying on that raft, striving with hands and feet, cross safely to the far shore.
mn22:13.13: Having crossed over and gone to the far shore, it might occur to him:
mn22:13.14: ‘This raft has been very helpful to me.
mn22:13.15: Relying on this raft, striving with hands and feet, I have crossed safely to the far shore.
mn22:13.16: Why don’t I lift this raft onto my head or carry it on my shoulder, and then go wherever I wish?’
mn22:13.18: would that man, by acting like that, be doing what should be done with that raft?”
mn22:13.2: Listen to that, pay close attention, I will speak.”
mn22:13.20: “And how, *bhikkhus*, would that man be doing what should be done with that raft?
mn22:13.21: Here, *bhikkhus*, having crossed over and gone to the far shore, it might occur to that man:
mn22:13.22: ‘This raft has been very helpful to me.
mn22:13.23: Relying on this raft, striving with hands and feet, I have crossed safely to the far shore.
mn22:13.24: Why don’t I pull this raft up onto dry land or sink it in the water, and then go wherever I wish?’
mn22:13.25: By acting like that, *bhikkhus*, that man would be doing what should be done with that raft.
mn22:13.26: In the same way, *bhikkhus*, I have taught the *dhamma* by the simile of a raft for the purpose of crossing over, not for the purpose of grasping.
mn22:13.5: “Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a man were traveling along a road.
mn22:13.6: He might see a great expanse of water, with this shore dangerous and fearful, and the far shore secure and free from fear;
mn22:13.7: but there would be no boat or bridge for going from this shore to the far shore.
mn22:13.8: Then it might occur to him:
mn22:13.9: ‘This is a great expanse of water. This shore is dangerous and fearful, and the far shore is secure and free from fear.
mn22:14.1: Having understood the *dhamma* by the simile of a raft, *bhikkhus*, even *dhammas* should be abandoned by you, let alone non-*dhammas*.
mn22:15.1: There are these six grounds for views, *bhikkhus*.
mn22:15.10: ‘That world is that self; after death I shall be permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change; I shall endure just so as long as eternity’—
mn22:15.11: he regards that too thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’
mn22:15.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, an uninstructed ordinary person, who has not seen the noble ones, who is unskilled in the noble *dhamma*, untrained in the noble *dhamma*, who has not seen true persons, who is unskilled in the *dhamma* of true persons, untrained in the *dhamma* of true persons,
mn22:15.4: regards *rūpa* thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn22:15.5: he regards *vedanā* thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn22:15.6: he regards *saññā* thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn22:15.7: he regards *saṅkhāras* thus: ‘These are mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn22:15.8: and whatever is seen, heard, sensed, cognized, attained, sought after, and explored by the mind, he regards that too thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn22:15.9: and whatever that ground for views may be—
mn22:16.1: But, *bhikkhus*, an instructed noble disciple, who has seen the noble ones, who is skilled in the noble *dhamma*, well trained in the noble *dhamma*, who has seen true persons, who is skilled in the *dhamma* of true persons, well trained in the *dhamma* of true persons,
mn22:16.2: regards *rūpa* thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.3: he regards *vedanā* thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.4: he regards *saññā* thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.5: he regards *saṅkhāras* thus: ‘These are not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.6: and whatever is seen, heard, sensed, cognized, attained, sought after, and explored by the mind, he regards that too thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.7: and whatever that ground for views may be—
mn22:16.8: ‘That world is that self; after death I shall be permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change; I shall endure just so as long as eternity’—
mn22:16.9: he regards that too thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn22:17.1: Seeing thus, he does not become anxious when there is nothing there.”
mn22:18.1: When this was said, a certain *bhikkhu* said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn22:18.2: “Could there be, *bhante*, anxiety when there is nothing there externally?”
mn22:18.5: ‘I had it; now I do not have that.
mn22:18.6: May I have it; but I do not get that.’
mn22:18.7: He sorrows, wears himself out, laments, beats his breast and cries, and falls into confusion.
mn22:18.8: In this way, *bhikkhu*, there is anxiety when there is nothing there externally.”
mn22:19.1: “But could there be, *bhante*, non-anxiety when there is nothing there externally?”
mn22:19.3: “Here, *bhikkhu*, for someone it does not occur thus:
mn22:19.4: ‘I had it; now I do not have that.
mn22:19.5: May I have it; but I do not get that.’
mn22:19.6: He does not sorrow, does not wear himself out, does not lament, does not beat his breast and cry, and does not fall into confusion.
mn22:19.7: In this way, *bhikkhu*, there is non-anxiety when there is nothing there externally.”
mn22:2.1: Now at that time, for a *bhikkhu* named Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, this bad view had arisen:
mn22:2.2: “I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā* like this: those *dhammas* said by the *Bhagavā* to be obstructive are not able to obstruct one who engages in them.”
mn22:2.4: “It seems that for the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, this bad view has arisen:
mn22:2.5: ‘I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā* like this: those *dhammas* said by the *Bhagavā* to be obstructive are not able to obstruct one who engages in them.’”
mn22:20.1: “Could there be, *bhante*, anxiety when there is nothing there internally?”
mn22:20.4: ‘That world is that self; after death I shall be permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change; I shall endure just so as long as eternity.’
mn22:20.5: He hears the *dhamma* being taught by the Tathāgata or a disciple of the Tathāgata for the uprooting of all underlying tendencies [*anusaya*] of grounds for views, their fixations, obsessions, adherences, and underlying tendencies; for the settling of all *saṅkhāras* [*sabbasaṅkhārasamathāya*]; for the relinquishment of all acquisitions; for the ending of *taṇhā*; for fading away; for cessation; for *nibbāna*.
mn22:20.7: ‘Surely I shall be annihilated, surely I shall perish, surely I shall no longer exist.’
mn22:20.8: He sorrows, wears himself out, laments, beats his breast and cries, and falls into confusion.
mn22:20.9: In this way, *bhikkhu*, there is anxiety when there is nothing there internally.”
mn22:21.1: “But could there be, *bhante*, non-anxiety when there is nothing there internally?”
mn22:21.3: “Here, *bhikkhu*, someone does not have this view:
mn22:21.4: ‘That world is that self; after death I shall be permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change; I shall endure just so as long as eternity.’
mn22:21.5: He hears the *dhamma* being taught by the Tathāgata or a disciple of the Tathāgata for the uprooting of all underlying tendencies [*anusaya*] of grounds for views, their fixations, obsessions, adherences, and underlying tendencies; for the settling of all *saṅkhāras* [*sabbasaṅkhārasamathāya*]; for the relinquishment of all acquisitions; for the ending of *taṇhā*; for fading away; for cessation; for *nibbāna*.
mn22:21.6: Then it does not occur to him:
mn22:21.7: ‘Surely I shall be annihilated, surely I shall perish, surely I shall no longer exist.’
mn22:21.8: He does not sorrow, does not wear himself out, does not lament, does not beat his breast and cry, and does not fall into confusion.
mn22:21.9: In this way, *bhikkhu*, there is non-anxiety when there is nothing there internally.
mn22:22.1: Would you take up, *bhikkhus*, a possession such that the possession of it would be permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change, and remain just so as long as eternity?
mn22:22.2: Do you see such a possession, *bhikkhus*, such that the possession of it would be permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change, and remain just so as long as eternity?”
mn22:22.5: I too, *bhikkhus*, do not see such a possession, such that the possession of it would be permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change, and remain just so as long as eternity.
mn22:23.1: Would you take up clinging/feeding on a doctrine of self [*attavādupādānaṁ*], such that, when that clinging/feeding [*upādāna*] is taken up, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and distress would not arise?
mn22:23.2: Do you see such clinging/feeding on a doctrine of self [*attavādupādānaṁ*], such that, when that clinging/feeding [*upādāna*] is taken up, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and distress would not arise?”
mn22:23.5: I too, *bhikkhus*, do not see such clinging/feeding on a doctrine of self [*attavādupādānaṁ*], such that, when that clinging/feeding [*upādāna*] is taken up, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and distress would not arise.
mn22:24.1: Would you rely on a support for views, such that, when relying on that support for views, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and distress would not arise?
mn22:24.2: Do you see such a support for views, such that, when relying on that support for views, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and distress would not arise?”
mn22:24.5: I too, *bhikkhus*, do not see such a support for views, such that, when relying on that support for views, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and distress would not arise.
mn22:25.1: “If there were a self, *bhikkhus*, would there be for me what belongs to self?”
mn22:25.3: “And if there were what belongs to self, *bhikkhus*, would there be for me a self?”
mn22:25.5: “But when self and what belongs to self are not truly and actually found, then that ground for views—
mn22:25.6: ‘That world is that self; after death I shall be permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change; I shall endure just so as long as eternity’—
mn22:25.7: isn’t that, *bhikkhus*, a complete and perfect fool’s doctrine?”
mn22:25.8: “How could it not be, *bhante*? It is indeed, *bhante*, a complete and perfect fool’s doctrine.”
mn22:26.15: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], is that *dukkha* or *sukha*?”
mn22:26.17: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *dukkha*, and subject to change, is it fitting to regard it thus—
mn22:26.18: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
mn22:26.4: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], is that *dukkha* or *sukha*?”
mn22:26.6: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *dukkha*, and subject to change, is it fitting to regard it thus—
mn22:26.7: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
mn22:27.1: “Therefore, *bhikkhus*, whatever *rūpa* there is—past, future, or present; internal or external; coarse or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near—all *rūpa* should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn22:27.5: whatever consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*] there is—past, future, or present; internal or external; coarse or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near—all consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*] should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn22:28.1: Seeing thus, *bhikkhus*, the instructed noble disciple becomes disenchanted with *rūpa*, becomes disenchanted with *vedanā*, becomes disenchanted with *saññā*, becomes disenchanted with *saṅkhāras*, becomes disenchanted with consciousness [*viññāṇasmiṁ*],
mn22:29.1: through disenchantment he becomes dispassionate; through dispassion he is liberated; when liberated, there is the knowledge: ‘Liberated.’
mn22:29.2: He understands: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’
mn22:3.1: Then those *bhikkhus* went to the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter. Having approached him, they said to him:
mn22:3.10: sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a lump of meat …
mn22:3.11: sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a grass torch …
mn22:3.12: sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a pit of glowing coals …
mn22:3.13: sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a dream …
mn22:3.14: sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like borrowed goods …
mn22:3.15: sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like fruits on a tree …
mn22:3.16: sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a butcher’s knife and block …
mn22:3.17: sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like spears and stakes …
mn22:3.18: sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a snake’s head, with much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.”
mn22:3.19: But even while the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, was being questioned, pressed, and admonished by those *bhikkhus*, he kept asserting that very same bad view, insisting on it, clinging to it, and holding to it:
mn22:3.2: “Is it really true, friend Ariṭṭha, that this bad view has arisen in you:
mn22:3.20: “That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, friends: those *dhammas* said by the *Bhagavā* to be obstructive are not able to obstruct one who engages in them.”
mn22:3.21: Since those *bhikkhus* were unable to draw the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, away from that bad view, they then went to the *Bhagavā*. Having approached, they paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, those *bhikkhus* said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn22:3.22: “*Bhante*, for the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, this bad view has arisen:
mn22:3.23: ‘I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā* like this: those *dhammas* said by the *Bhagavā* to be obstructive are not able to obstruct one who engages in them.’
mn22:3.25: ‘It seems that for the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, this bad view has arisen—
mn22:3.26: I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā* like this: those *dhammas* said by the *Bhagavā* to be obstructive are not able to obstruct one who engages in them.’
mn22:3.27: Then, *bhante*, we went to the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter. Having approached him, we said to him:
mn22:3.28: ‘Is it really true, friend Ariṭṭha, that this bad view has arisen in you—
mn22:3.29: I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā* like this: those *dhammas* said by the *Bhagavā* to be obstructive are not able to obstruct one who engages in them’?
mn22:3.3: ‘I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā* like this: those *dhammas* said by the *Bhagavā* to be obstructive are not able to obstruct one who engages in them’?”
mn22:3.30: When this was said, *bhante*, the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, said this to us:
mn22:3.31: ‘That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, friends: those *dhammas* said by the *Bhagavā* to be obstructive are not able to obstruct one who engages in them.’
mn22:3.32: Then, *bhante*, wanting to draw the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, away from that bad view, we questioned him, pressed him, and admonished him:
mn22:3.33: ‘Do not say that, friend Ariṭṭha, do not misrepresent the *Bhagavā*; for it is not good to misrepresent the *Bhagavā*, and the *Bhagavā* would not say that.
mn22:3.34: In many ways, friend Ariṭṭha, obstructive *dhammas* have been called obstructive by the *Bhagavā*, and they are indeed enough to obstruct one who engages in them.
mn22:3.35: Sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to give little gratification, much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.
mn22:3.36: Sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a skeleton …
mn22:3.37: sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a snake’s head, with much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.’
mn22:3.38: But even while the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, was being questioned, pressed, and admonished by us, he kept asserting that very same bad view, insisting on it, clinging to it, and holding to it:
mn22:3.39: ‘That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, friends: those *dhammas* said by the *Bhagavā* to be obstructive are not able to obstruct one who engages in them.’
mn22:3.4: “That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, friends: those *dhammas* said by the *Bhagavā* to be obstructive are not able to obstruct one who engages in them.”
mn22:3.5: Then those *bhikkhus*, wanting to draw the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, away from that bad view, questioned him, pressed him, and admonished him:
mn22:3.6: “Do not say that, friend Ariṭṭha, do not misrepresent the *Bhagavā*; for it is not good to misrepresent the *Bhagavā*, and the *Bhagavā* would not say that.
mn22:3.7: In many ways, friend Ariṭṭha, obstructive *dhammas* have been called obstructive by the *Bhagavā*, and they are indeed enough to obstruct one who engages in them.
mn22:3.8: Sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to give little gratification, much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.
mn22:3.9: Sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a skeleton …
mn22:30.1: This, *bhikkhus*, is called a *bhikkhu* with the crossbar lifted, a *bhikkhu* with the moat filled in, a *bhikkhu* with the boundary post uprooted, a *bhikkhu* with the bolt removed, a noble one with banner lowered, burden laid down, detached.
mn22:31.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, is a *bhikkhu* one with the crossbar lifted?
mn22:31.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu’s* blindness [*avijjā*] has been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, done away with, not liable to arise in the future.
mn22:31.3: In this way, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is one with the crossbar lifted.
mn22:32.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, is a *bhikkhu* one with the moat filled in?
mn22:32.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, for a *bhikkhu* the round of birth and wandering-on that leads to renewed existence has been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, done away with, not liable to arise in the future.
mn22:32.3: In this way, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is one with the moat filled in.
mn22:33.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, is a *bhikkhu* one with the boundary post uprooted?
mn22:33.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu’s* *taṇhā* has been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, done away with, not liable to arise in the future.
mn22:33.3: In this way, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is one with the boundary post uprooted.
mn22:34.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, is a *bhikkhu* one with the bolt removed?
mn22:34.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, for a *bhikkhu* the five lower fetters have been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, done away with, not liable to arise in the future.
mn22:34.3: In this way, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is one with the bolt removed.
mn22:35.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, is a *bhikkhu* a noble one with banner lowered, burden laid down, detached?
mn22:35.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, for a *bhikkhu* the conceit ‘I am’ has been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, done away with, not liable to arise in the future.
mn22:35.3: In this way, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is a noble one with banner lowered, burden laid down, detached.
mn22:36.1: For such a *bhikkhu*, *bhikkhus*, whose mind [*citta*] is thus liberated, the devas with Inda, with Brahmā, and with Pajāpati, while searching, do not find:
mn22:36.2: ‘The consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*] of the Tathāgata is supported by this.’
mn22:36.4: Because, *bhikkhus*, I say that the Tathāgata is untraceable here and now.
mn22:37.1: Though I speak thus and declare thus, *bhikkhus*, some ascetics and brahmins misrepresent me with what is unreal, empty, false, and untrue:
mn22:37.2: ‘The ascetic Gotama is one who teaches eradication; he declares the annihilation, destruction, and extermination of an existing being.’
mn22:37.3: But that is not what I am, *bhikkhus*, and that is not what I say, though those good ascetics and brahmins misrepresent me with what is unreal, empty, false, and untrue:
mn22:37.4: ‘The ascetic Gotama is one who teaches eradication; he declares the annihilation, destruction, and extermination of an existing being.’
mn22:37.5: Formerly and now, *bhikkhus*, I make known only *dukkha* and the cessation of *dukkha*.
mn22:37.6: If, *bhikkhus*, others abuse, revile, provoke, and harass the Tathāgata over that, then for the Tathāgata there is no resentment, no displeasure, no dissatisfaction of mind.
mn22:38.1: And if, *bhikkhus*, others honor, respect, esteem, and venerate the Tathāgata over that, then for the Tathāgata there is no joy, no gladness, no elation of mind.
mn22:38.2: And if, *bhikkhus*, others honor, respect, esteem, and venerate the Tathāgata, then this occurs to the Tathāgata:
mn22:38.3: ‘Such actions are being done for me with regard to what was formerly fully understood.’
mn22:39.1: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, if others abuse, revile, provoke, and harass you, then you should not make resentment, displeasure, or dissatisfaction of mind over that.
mn22:39.2: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, if others honor, respect, esteem, and venerate you, then you should not make joy, gladness, or elation of mind over that.
mn22:39.3: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, if others honor, respect, esteem, and venerate you, then this should occur to you:
mn22:39.4: ‘Such actions are being done for me with regard to what was formerly fully understood.’
mn22:4.1: Since we were unable, *bhante*, to draw the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, away from that bad view, we have reported this matter to the *Bhagavā*.”
mn22:40.1: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, whatever is not yours, abandon it;
mn22:40.2: when abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.10: Consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*], *bhikkhus*, is not yours. Abandon it;
mn22:41.11: when abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.13: if people were to carry off the grass, sticks, branches, and leaves in this Jeta’s Grove, or burn them, or do with them whatever they like,
mn22:41.15: ‘People are carrying us off, or burning us, or doing with us whatever they like’?”
mn22:41.18: “Because, *bhante*, that is not our self or what belongs to our self.”
mn22:41.19: “In the same way, *bhikkhus*, whatever is not yours, abandon it;
mn22:41.20: when abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.23: when abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.27: consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*], *bhikkhus*, is not yours. Abandon it;
mn22:41.28: when abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.3: when abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.5: when abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.7: when abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.9: when abandoned by you, they will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:42.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, the *dhamma* has been well proclaimed by me—plain, open, clear, with the patchwork cut away.
mn22:42.2: When the *dhamma* has thus been well proclaimed by me—plain, open, clear, with the patchwork cut away—those *bhikkhus* who are *arahants*, with *āsavas* destroyed [*khīṇāsavā*], who have lived the holy life, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, reached their own goal, with the fetters of existence entirely destroyed, completely liberated through right knowing—there is no cycle left by which they could be designated.
mn22:43.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, the *dhamma* has been well proclaimed by me—plain, open, clear, with the patchwork cut away.
mn22:43.2: When the *dhamma* has thus been well proclaimed by me—plain, open, clear, with the patchwork cut away—those *bhikkhus* whose five lower fetters have been abandoned, all of them are spontaneously reborn, there attaining final *nibbāna*, not liable to return from that world.
mn22:44.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, the *dhamma* has been well proclaimed by me—plain, open, clear, with the patchwork cut away.
mn22:44.2: When the *dhamma* has thus been well proclaimed by me—plain, open, clear, with the patchwork cut away—those *bhikkhus* whose three fetters have been abandoned, with greed, hate, and delusion thinned out, all of them are once-returners, who, having come back to this world just once more, will make an end of *dukkha*.
mn22:45.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, the *dhamma* has been well proclaimed by me—plain, open, clear, with the patchwork cut away.
mn22:45.2: When the *dhamma* has thus been well proclaimed by me—plain, open, clear, with the patchwork cut away—those *bhikkhus* whose three fetters have been abandoned, all of them are stream-enterers, not liable to downfall, fixed, headed for full awakening.
mn22:46.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, the *dhamma* has been well proclaimed by me—plain, open, clear, with the patchwork cut away.
mn22:46.2: When the *dhamma* has thus been well proclaimed by me—plain, open, clear, with the patchwork cut away—those *bhikkhus* who follow the *dhamma* and those who follow faith, all of them are headed for full awakening.
mn22:47.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, the *dhamma* has been well proclaimed by me—plain, open, clear, with the patchwork cut away.
mn22:47.2: When the *dhamma* has thus been well proclaimed by me—plain, open, clear, with the patchwork cut away—those who have mere faith in me, mere love for me, all of them are headed for heaven.”
mn22:47.4: Delighted, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn22:47.5: The second is finished: The Simile of the Snake.
mn22:5.2: “Come, *bhikkhu*, address the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, in my name:
mn22:5.4: “Yes, *bhante*,” that *bhikkhu* replied to the *Bhagavā*. Then he went to the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter. Having approached him, he said:
mn22:5.6: “Yes, friend,” replied the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter. Then he went to the *Bhagavā*. Having approached, he paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, the *Bhagavā* said this to the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter:
mn22:5.7: “Is it really true, Ariṭṭha, that this bad view has arisen in you:
mn22:5.8: ‘I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā* like this: those *dhammas* said by the *Bhagavā* to be obstructive are not able to obstruct one who engages in them’?”
mn22:5.9: “That is exactly how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*: ‘those *dhammas* said by the *Bhagavā* to be obstructive are not able to obstruct one who engages in them.’”
mn22:6.1: “To whom, foolish man, do you understand me to have taught the *dhamma* in that way?
mn22:6.10: sensual pleasures have been said by me to be like fruits on a tree …
mn22:6.11: sensual pleasures have been said by me to be like a butcher’s knife and block …
mn22:6.12: sensual pleasures have been said by me to be like spears and stakes …
mn22:6.13: sensual pleasures have been said by me to be like a snake’s head, with much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.
mn22:6.14: And yet you, foolish man, by your own wrong grasp have both misrepresented us and harmed yourself, and you generate much demerit.
mn22:6.15: That will be for your harm and *dukkha* for a long time, foolish man.”
mn22:6.2: Have I not, foolish man, in many ways said that obstructive *dhammas* are obstructive? And they are indeed enough to obstruct one who engages in them.
mn22:6.3: Sensual pleasures have been said by me to give little gratification, much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.
mn22:6.4: Sensual pleasures have been said by me to be like a skeleton …
mn22:6.5: sensual pleasures have been said by me to be like a lump of meat …
mn22:6.6: sensual pleasures have been said by me to be like a grass torch …
mn22:6.7: sensual pleasures have been said by me to be like a pit of glowing coals …
mn22:6.8: sensual pleasures have been said by me to be like a dream …
mn22:6.9: sensual pleasures have been said by me to be like borrowed goods …
mn22:7.3: has this *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, even become warm in this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*]?”
mn22:7.6: When this was said, the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, sat silent, embarrassed, shoulders drooping, downcast, brooding, without a reply.
mn22:7.7: Then the *Bhagavā*, knowing that the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, was silent, embarrassed, shoulders drooping, downcast, brooding, and without a reply, said to him:
mn22:7.8: “You will be recognized, foolish man, by this very own bad view of yours.
mn22:7.9: Now I shall question the *bhikkhus* about this.”
mn22:8.10: For in many ways, *bhikkhus*, obstructive *dhammas* have been said by me, and they are indeed enough to obstruct one who engages in them.
mn22:8.11: Sensual pleasures have been said by me to give little gratification, much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.
mn22:8.12: Sensual pleasures have been said by me to be like a skeleton …
mn22:8.13: sensual pleasures have been said by me to be like a snake’s head, with much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.
mn22:8.14: But this *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, by his own wrong grasp both misrepresents us and harms himself and generates much demerit.
mn22:8.15: That will be for the harm and *dukkha* of that foolish man for a long time.
mn22:8.2: “Do you too understand the *dhamma* taught by me in the way that this *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-hunter, by his own wrong grasp both misrepresents us and harms himself and generates much demerit?”
mn22:8.4: For in many ways, *bhante*, obstructive *dhammas* have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be obstructive;
mn22:8.5: and they are indeed enough to obstruct one who engages in them.
mn22:8.6: Sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to give little gratification, much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.
mn22:8.7: Sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a skeleton …
mn22:8.8: sensual pleasures have been said by the *Bhagavā* to be like a snake’s head, with much *dukkha*, much distress; the drawback here is greater.”
mn22:8.9: “*Sādhu*, *sādhu*, *bhikkhus*; indeed, *bhikkhus*, you understand the *dhamma* taught by me in this way.
mn22:9.1: That one could engage in sensual pleasures apart from sensual pleasures, apart from the perception of sensual pleasures, apart from thoughts of sensual pleasures [*kāmavitakkehi*]—that is impossible.
